name: Skinwalker
description: Skinwalkers, known as *yee naaldlooshii* ('by means of it, it goes on all fours') in Navajo, are malevolent shapeshifting witches in Navajo folklore. They are considered powerful and dangerous figures who can transform into animals, often wolves, coyotes, foxes, eagles, owls, or other creatures. They are said to be former medicine men or women who have abused their powers for evil. Skinwalkers are believed to possess supernatural abilities, including the power to inflict illness, cause misfortune, and even kill. They are often associated with grave robbing and other dark practices, and their existence inspires fear and dread within the Navajo community. These entities violate cultural taboos and represent a perversion of sacred knowledge, making them a constant source of anxiety and vigilance. The belief in skinwalkers is deeply ingrained in Navajo culture, serving as a cautionary tale about the misuse of power and the importance of adhering to traditional values.
subtitle: Malevolent shapeshifting witch of Navajo folklore.
appearance: Skinwalkers can appear in various animal forms, most commonly wolves, coyotes, foxes, eagles, or owls. When in human form, they might be recognized by their unsettling eyes or strange behavior. Some accounts describe them wearing the skins of animals they transform into, although this is more of a metaphorical representation of their connection to the animal world and their violation of natural order.
habitat: Skinwalkers are said to inhabit the Navajo Nation, particularly in remote and desolate areas. They are associated with places considered to be spiritually powerful or tainted, such as burial grounds and areas where sacred ceremonies have been desecrated.
behavior: Skinwalkers are believed to be active primarily at night, preying on the vulnerable and spreading fear. They are said to mimic the sounds of animals or humans to lure victims. Their behavior is characterized by malice, cruelty, and a disregard for traditional Navajo values. They actively seek to disrupt harmony and bring chaos to communities.

keyMyths.title: The Skinwalker and the Stolen Sheep
keyMyths.description: A family discovers that their sheep are being stolen each night. They suspect a skinwalker is responsible. A medicine man performs a ceremony to protect the flock. The next morning, a neighbor is found injured, revealing him to be the skinwalker who was trying to steal the sheep. The ceremony weakened him, allowing the family to identify him.

keyMyths.title: The Skinwalker Mimicking a Loved One
keyMyths.description: A lone traveler hears the voice of their deceased relative calling out in the night. Drawn by the familiar voice, they venture into the darkness, only to be confronted by a skinwalker in animal form. Realizing the deception, the traveler manages to escape, but is left deeply shaken by the encounter. This story highlights the skinwalker's ability to manipulate and deceive.

keyMyths.title: The Skinwalker and the Cursed Home
keyMyths.description: A family moves into a new home, only to be plagued by strange occurrences and illnesses. They discover that the house was built on a burial ground, attracting the attention of a skinwalker. A medicine man is called in to cleanse the home and banish the evil spirit, restoring peace to the family. This story emphasizes the importance of respecting sacred sites.

sources.author: Lévi-Strauss, Claude
sources.description: Provides a framework for understanding the symbolic structures and cultural meanings embedded in Navajo folklore, including the belief in skinwalkers.
sources.text: Provides a framework for understanding the symbolic structures and cultural meanings embedded in Navajo folklore, including the belief in skinwalkers.

sources.author: Toelken, Barre
sources.description: Offers insights into the role of folklore in shaping cultural identity and transmitting traditional knowledge within the Navajo community, which is crucial to understanding the significance of skinwalkers.

sources.author: Roessel, Ruth
sources.work: Navajo Stories of the Long Walk Period
sources.description: While not directly about skinwalkers, this work provides valuable context about Navajo history and cultural resilience, helping to understand the anxieties reflected in skinwalker beliefs.
sources.text: While not directly about skinwalkers, this work provides valuable context about Navajo history and cultural resilience, helping to understand the anxieties reflected in skinwalker beliefs.

sources.author: Williamson, Ray A.
sources.work: Living the Sky: The Cosmos of the American Indian
sources.description: Provides context on the spiritual beliefs of Indigenous peoples of the Americas, and a deeper understanding of the Navajo's connection to nature and the spirit world, which is essential for understanding the place of Skinwalkers in their belief system.
sources.text: Provides context on the spiritual beliefs of Indigenous peoples of the Americas, and a deeper understanding of the Navajo's connection to nature and the spirit world, which is essential for understanding the place of Skinwalkers in their belief system.
